notes: searches: ~11, clusters: 9. Verified all 91 needs_verification claims plus control sample of 3 needs_verification:false claims (GC-09-002, GC-09-034, GC-09-093). Chapter follows D'Aubigne's History of the Reformation (footnotes 241-260, 262-263) and Wylie's History of Protestantism (footnote 261); attributed quotes trace to these cited period sources and their substance is consistent with modern Zwingli scholarship (Schaff, Britannica, Wikipedia, World History Encyclopedia). No fabricated events or quotes found; no contradicted/anachronistic verdicts warranted. Low confidence limited to bare D'Aubigne anecdote-quotes not independently locatable.

claims.quote: To the unlearned, toiling fishermen of Galilee was the call addressed, “Follow Me, and I will make you fishers of men.”
claims.claim_text: Jesus called unlearned, toiling fishermen of Galilee to follow him, saying, "Follow Me, and I will make you fishers of men."

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Direct quotation of Matthew 4:19 (KJV), addressed to the Galilean fishermen Peter and Andrew. Textbook-level biblical fact reproduced verbatim.

claims.quote: So in the days of the Great Reformation. The leading Reformers were men from humble life,—men who were most free of any of their time from pride of rank, and from the influence of bigotry and priestcraft.
claims.claim_text: The leading Protestant Reformers of the sixteenth century came from humble social backgrounds and were largely free of aristocratic pride and clerical bigotry.

verification.rationale: Control-sample re-check of an extractor 'needs_verification:false' claim. Dominant character is a value-laden generalization. It does embed a partly-checkable sub-claim (several leading reformers had humble origins: Zwingli a farmer/herdsman's son, Luther a miner's son, Melanchthon an armorer's son) which is true for some but not all (Calvin was a notary/administrator's son), but 'most free of any of their time from pride of rank and bigotry' is a comparative value judgment that no source can adjudicate as a single proposition. Extractor blind-spot noted: the interpretation smuggles a diffuse factual assertion, but it is too broad to verify or contradict cleanly.

claims.quote: A few weeks after the birth of Luther in a miner’s cabin in Saxony, Ulric Zwingle was born in a herdsman’s cottage among the Alps.
claims.claim_text: Martin Luther was born in a miner's cabin in Saxony.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Luther was born 10 November 1483 in Eisleben, in Saxony; his father Hans Luther was a copper miner. Textbook-level biographical fact.

claims.quote: A few weeks after the birth of Luther in a miner’s cabin in Saxony, Ulric Zwingle was born in a herdsman’s cottage among the Alps.
claims.claim_text: Ulrich Zwingli was born a few weeks after Martin Luther, in a herdsman's cottage in the Alps.

verification.rationale: Zwingli was born 1 January 1484 in Wildhaus, in the alpine Toggenburg valley, to a family of farmers/herdsmen; his father was the chief local magistrate but the household was a mountain farming one. That is roughly seven weeks after Luther's birth (10 Nov 1483), consistent with 'a few weeks after.' GC does not name Wildhaus (extractor note correct); the wording 'herdsman's cottage among the Alps' matches the alpine farming origin.

claims.quote: At the age of thirteen he went to Bern, which then possessed the most distinguished school in Switzerland.
claims.claim_text: At age thirteen, Ulrich Zwingli went to Bern to attend what was then the most distinguished school in Switzerland.

verification.rationale: After primary schooling with his uncle at Weesen and secondary schooling at Basel from age ten, Zwingli moved (c. age 13, ~1496-98) to Bern to study under the humanist Heinrich Wolflin (Lupulus), whose Latin/humanist school was among the leading schools in the Confederation. The age fits the documented sequence; 'most distinguished school in Switzerland' is a mild superlative that traces to D'Aubigne but is defensible for Wolflin's humanist school.

claims.quote: Determined efforts were put forth by the friars to allure him into a monastery.
claims.claim_text: Friars at Bern made determined efforts to induce the young Zwingli to enter a monastery.

verification.rationale: Standard biographies record that at Bern the Dominicans, impressed by young Zwingli's exceptional musical/singing gifts, tried to draw him into their order/convent; his family intervened. The recruitment attempt is well attested.

claims.quote: The Dominican and Franciscan monks were in rivalry for popular favor.
claims.claim_text: At Bern, the Dominican and Franciscan monastic orders competed with one another for popular favor.

verification.rationale: Mendicant rivalry between Dominicans and Franciscans (over relics, cult, and popular devotion) is a well-known feature of the late-medieval church, and Bern was the scene of the notorious Jetzer affair (1507-09) in which the Dominicans staged fraudulent miracles precisely to compete for popular favor. The specific 'rivalry at Bern' framing traces to D'Aubigne but is historically grounded.

claims.quote: The Dominicans of Bern saw that if they could win this talented young scholar, they would secure both gain and honor.
claims.claim_text: The Dominicans at Bern sought to recruit Zwingli into their order believing his talents would bring the order financial gain and honor.
claims.extractor_note: Ascribed motive; borderline interpretive but presented as a historical causal assertion.

verification.rationale: The Dominicans' interest in the young Zwingli because of his talents (especially music) is documented; the specific ascription of a gain-and-honor motive is D'Aubigne's causal framing, plausible given mendicant orders' use of gifted preachers/singers to draw crowds and revenue, but not independently attested as their stated intent.

claims.quote: By deceit and flattery they endeavored to induce Zwingle to enter their convent.
claims.claim_text: The Dominican friars at Bern used deceit and flattery in an attempt to induce Zwingli to enter their convent.

verification.rationale: The recruitment attempt is attested; the pejorative characterization 'deceit and flattery' is D'Aubigne's (Protestant) coloring of the Dominicans' enticement of the youth, but the underlying event (their trying to lure him in) is confirmed by standard biographies.

claims.quote: Luther, while a student at school, had buried himself in a convent cell, and he would have been lost to the world had not God’s providence released him.
claims.claim_text: Martin Luther, while a student, entered a convent cell.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Luther entered the Augustinian friary at Erfurt in 1505, abandoning his legal studies after the Stotternheim thunderstorm vow. That he 'buried himself in a convent cell' as a young student is a textbook biographical fact.

claims.quote: Luther, while a student at school, had buried himself in a convent cell, and he would have been lost to the world had not God’s providence released him.
claims.claim_text: Divine providence released Martin Luther from being lost to the world in a convent.

verification.rationale: Theological/providential interpretation of Luther's departure from monastic life. Divine agency is not empirically testable.

claims.quote: Providentially his father received information of the designs of the friars.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli's father received information about the friars' plan to draw his son into a monastery.

verification.rationale: Biographies record that Zwingli's family (father and uncle) learned of and dissuaded the Dominican attempt, removing him from Bern before he could be professed. The family intervention is confirmed; the specific channel of 'information' is D'Aubigne's narrative detail.

claims.quote: Providentially his father received information of the designs of the friars.
claims.claim_text: It was providential (an act of divine providence) that Zwingli's father learned of the friars' designs.

verification.rationale: Ascription of divine providence to the father's discovery. Not empirically testable.

claims.quote: He saw that his future usefulness was at stake, and directed him to return home without delay.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli's father directed him to return home immediately from Bern to prevent him from entering a monastery.

verification.rationale: The family removed Zwingli from Bern to prevent his profession as a Dominican; modern accounts have him proceed to Vienna (1498) after Bern rather than only home-then-Basel, so GC compresses the itinerary, but the core event (family pulling him out to stop the monastic recruitment) is attested.

claims.quote: The command was obeyed; but the youth could not be long content in his native valley, and he soon resumed his studies, repairing, after a time, to Basel.
claims.claim_text: Ulrich Zwingli resumed his studies at Basel after returning home from Bern.

verification.rationale: Zwingli studied at the University of Basel, taking his Master of Arts (Magister) there in 1506. GC compresses the itinerary (it omits the University of Vienna, c. 1498-1502, which came before this Basel period), but the claim that he resumed studies at Basel is correct.

claims.quote: It was here that Zwingle first heard the gospel of God’s free grace.
claims.claim_text: Ulrich Zwingli first heard the doctrine of salvation by free grace while studying at Basel.

verification.rationale: At Basel Zwingli studied under Thomas Wyttenbach, who taught that Christ's death was the sole price of the remission of sins and criticized indulgences. Attributing Zwingli's first exposure to the evangelical/free-grace emphasis to this Basel period is consistent with the record, though it reflects an interpretive framing of when he 'first heard' it.

sources.title: Thomas Wyttenbach: Zwingli's Teacher and Mentor

claims.quote: Wittembach, a teacher of the ancient languages, had, while studying Greek and Hebrew, been led to the Holy Scriptures, and thus rays of divine light were shed into the minds of the students under his instruction.
claims.claim_text: Wittembach, a teacher of ancient languages at Basel, was led to the study of the Holy Scriptures through his study of Greek and Hebrew, and conveyed this understanding to his students, including Zwingli.

verification.rationale: Thomas Wyttenbach (1472-1526) lectured at Basel c. 1505-07 and was Zwingli's teacher; he introduced humanist/biblical study, teaching that scholastic theology must give way to the study of the New Testament and the Fathers. Describing him as a teacher who turned students toward Scripture is accurate ('teacher of the ancient languages' aptly captures his humanist Greek/biblical orientation).

sources.title: Thomas Wyttenbach: Zwingli's Teacher and Mentor

claims.quote: He declared that there was a truth more ancient, and of infinitely greater worth, than the theories taught by schoolmen and philosophers.
claims.claim_text: Wittembach taught his students at Basel that there was a truth more ancient and of greater worth than scholastic theories.

verification.rationale: Consistent with the documented teaching of Wyttenbach that scholastic theology must give way to the purer theology of the early Fathers and to study of the New Testament. The specific phrasing traces to D'Aubigne but matches the substance of Wyttenbach's known position.

sources.title: Thomas Wyttenbach: Zwingli's Teacher and Mentor

claims.quote: This ancient truth was that the death of Christ is the sinner’s only ransom.
claims.claim_text: Wittembach taught that the death of Christ is the sinner's only ransom.

verification.rationale: Wyttenbach is recorded as teaching that 'the death of Christ was the sole price of the remission of sins' and that faith is the key that unlocks that remission — the exact doctrine GC ascribes to him. Zwingli himself later credited Wyttenbach with this teaching.

sources.title: Thomas Wyttenbach: Zwingli's Teacher and Mentor

claims.quote: His first field of labor was in an Alpine parish, not far distant from his native valley.
claims.claim_text: Ulrich Zwingli's first pastoral appointment was in an Alpine parish near his native valley.

verification.rationale: Zwingli's first ecclesiastical post was the pastorate of Glarus (1506), an alpine town in the Swiss cantons and in the same general alpine region as his native Toggenburg. 'Not far distant' is loose (Glarus is a canton away) but reasonable.

claims.quote: Having received ordination as a priest, he “devoted himself with his whole soul to the search after divine truth; for he was well aware,” says a fellow-reformer, “how much he must know to whom the flock of Christ is entrusted.”(242)
claims.claim_text: A fellow-reformer stated that after his ordination as a priest, Zwingli devoted himself wholly to the search for divine truth, aware of how much he needed to know as a pastor.

verification.rationale: The 'fellow-reformer' is Zwingli's early biographer Oswald Myconius (Vita Zwinglii). The quotation reaches GC via D'Aubigne (footnote 242); its substance — Zwingli's diligent self-study after ordination — is consistent with the documented record of his Glarus years, during which he taught himself Greek and studied the classics and Scripture intensively.

claims.quote: He submitted himself to the Bible as the word of God, the only sufficient, infallible rule.
claims.claim_text: Ulrich Zwingli held the Bible to be the sole sufficient, infallible rule of faith.

verification.rationale: Zwingli's commitment to Scripture as the sole and sufficient authority (sola scriptura) is a central, well-documented feature of his theology and his Zurich reform program.

claims.quote: “The Scriptures,” said Zwingle, “come from God, not from man, and even that God who enlightens will give thee to understand that the speech comes from God. The word of God ... cannot fail; it is bright, it teaches itself, it discloses itself, it illumines the soul with all salvation and grace, comforts it in God, humbles it, so that it loses and even forfeits itself, and embraces God.”(243)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted as stating that the Scriptures come from God, not man, and that God's word illumines the soul with salvation and grace.

verification.rationale: The passage comes to GC through D'Aubigne (footnote 243) and reflects Zwingli's treatise 'Of the Clarity and Certainty of the Word of God' (1522), whose thesis is precisely that Scripture is divine, self-authenticating, and enlightens the soul. The substance matches Zwingli's known writing; exact wording is D'Aubigne's rendering.

claims.quote: “When ... I began to give myself wholly up to the Holy Scriptures, philosophy and theology (scholastic) would always keep suggesting quarrels to me. At last I came to this, that I thought, ‘Thou must let all that lie, and learn the meaning of God purely out of His own simple word.’ Then I began to ask God for His light, and the Scriptures began to be much easier to me.”(244)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli wrote that when he devoted himself wholly to the Holy Scriptures, scholastic philosophy and theology kept suggesting quarrels, until he resolved to learn the meaning of God purely from Scripture and asked God for light, after which the Scriptures became easier to understand.

verification.rationale: Autobiographical statement transmitted via D'Aubigne (footnote 244). It matches the documented turning point (c. 1515-16) when Zwingli set aside scholasticism to read Scripture directly, using Erasmus's Greek New Testament. Substance consistent with the record; exact wording is the period translation.

claims.quote: “If Luther preaches Christ,” said the Swiss Reformer, “he does what I am doing. Those whom he has brought to Christ are more numerous than those whom I have led. But this matters not. I will bear no other name than that of Christ, whose soldier I am, and who alone is my chief. Never has one single word been written by me to Luther, nor by Luther to me. And why?... That it might be shown how much the Spirit of God is in unison with itself, since both of us, without any collusion, teach the doctrine of Christ with such uniformity.”(245)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted stating that if Luther preaches Christ, he does what Zwingli is doing, and that Luther has brought more people to Christ than Zwingli has.

verification.rationale: This is a genuine and much-cited Zwingli statement (from his 1527 apology, insisting he was not a 'Lutheran' and had learned the doctrine of Christ from the Word of God, not from Luther). The 'If Luther preaches Christ, he does the same thing as I do' declaration is well attested in Zwingli scholarship.

claims.quote: Never has one single word been written by me to Luther, nor by Luther to me.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli claimed that he and Luther had never exchanged a single written word with each other.

verification.rationale: This is Zwingli's own assertion (part of the quotation above), made to stress his independence from Luther. Historically the two had no significant direct correspondence in the early reform period; direct letters were not a feature of their relationship. GC reports it accurately as Zwingli's claim.

claims.quote: The doctrine preached by Zwingle was not received from Luther. It was the doctrine of Christ.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli's reformation doctrine developed independently of, and was not received from, Luther's teaching.

verification.rationale: Scholarly consensus holds that Zwingli developed his reform independently, shaped chiefly by Erasmian humanism and his own Scripture study; he began evangelical preaching around 1516, before Luther's 95 Theses (1517) had reached Switzerland, and he consistently denied dependence on Luther.

claims.quote: In 1516 Zwingle was invited to become a preacher in the convent at Einsiedeln.
claims.claim_text: In 1516, Ulrich Zwingli was invited to become a preacher at the convent of Einsiedeln.

verification.rationale: Zwingli took up the post of people's priest/preacher at the Benedictine abbey of Einsiedeln in 1516, leaving Glarus. Date and post are well documented.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: Among the chief attractions of Einsiedeln was an image of the Virgin which was said to have the power of working miracles.
claims.claim_text: The convent at Einsiedeln housed an image of the Virgin reputed to have miracle-working power, which drew pilgrims.

verification.rationale: Einsiedeln Abbey is famous for its Black Madonna (Our Lady of Einsiedeln), a Marian image that was and remains a major pilgrimage draw and reputed to work miracles.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: Above the gateway of the convent was the inscription, “Here a plenary remission of sins may be obtained.”(246)
claims.claim_text: An inscription above the gateway of the Einsiedeln convent read, "Here a plenary remission of sins may be obtained."

verification.rationale: Einsiedeln offered a famous plenary indulgence tied to the legend of the 'Angelic Consecration' (Engelweihe) of its chapel, and pilgrims came for the complete remission of sins. The specific inscription over the gate is reported by D'Aubigne (footnote 246) and Schaff's account of Zwingli at Einsiedeln; the plenary-indulgence claim of the shrine is historically well established even if the exact gate inscription rests on the period sources.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: Pilgrims at all seasons resorted to the shrine of the Virgin, but at the great yearly festival of its consecration, multitudes came from all parts of Switzerland, and even from France and Germany.
claims.claim_text: At the annual consecration festival of the Einsiedeln shrine, multitudes of pilgrims came from all parts of Switzerland, France, and Germany.

verification.rationale: Einsiedeln was one of the foremost international pilgrimage sites of the region; the annual Engelweihe (consecration) festival drew large crowds from across the Swiss cantons and neighboring lands. The reach 'Switzerland, France, and Germany' is consistent with its status; specific magnitude is not quantified in sources.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: “Do not imagine,” he said, “that God is in this temple more than in any other part of creation. Whatever be the country in which you dwell, God is around you, and hears you.... Can unprofitable works, long pilgrimages, offerings, images, the invocation of the Virgin or of the saints, secure for you the grace of God?... What avails the multitude of words with which we embody our prayers? What efficacy has a glossy cowl, a smooth-shorn head, a long and flowing robe, or gold-embroidered slippers?... God looks at the heart, and our hearts are far from Him.”(247)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted at Einsiedeln preaching that God is not present in the shrine more than elsewhere, and that pilgrimages, offerings, images, and invocation of the Virgin or saints cannot secure God's grace.

verification.rationale: The passage reaches GC via D'Aubigne (footnote 247). It is documented that Zwingli preached at Einsiedeln against pilgrimages, image-veneration, and the notion of localized grace at the shrine; the substance of the quotation matches this attested preaching.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: “Christ,” he said, “who was once offered upon the cross, is the sacrifice and victim, that had made satisfaction for the sins of believers to all eternity.”(247)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted stating that Christ, once offered on the cross, is the sacrifice that made eternal satisfaction for the sins of believers.

verification.rationale: Shares footnote 247 with the preceding quotation; from D'Aubigne. The single, all-sufficient, once-for-all sacrifice of Christ is a core element of Zwingli's soteriology, so the substance is consistent with his teaching.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: They were satisfied with the old way to heaven which Rome had marked out for them. They shrank from the perplexity of searching for anything better.
claims.claim_text: Many listeners at Einsiedeln rejected Zwingli's message of free grace, preferring the established practices prescribed by Rome.
claims.extractor_note: General characterization of audience reaction, not a specific checkable event.

verification.rationale: Control-sample re-check of an extractor 'needs_verification:false' claim. This is a homiletic generalization about listeners' inner motives ('satisfied,' 'shrank from perplexity') with no discrete checkable content. Correctly left unverified; confirmed unverifiable.

claims.quote: The truth was thus carried from hamlet to hamlet, from town to town, and the number of pilgrims to the Virgin’s shrine greatly lessened.
claims.claim_text: As a result of Zwingli's preaching at Einsiedeln, the number of pilgrims to the shrine of the Virgin there greatly lessened.

verification.rationale: Traditional Reformation historiography (Schaff, D'Aubigne) reports that Zwingli's anti-pilgrimage preaching at Einsiedeln discouraged pilgrims and spread reform ideas outward. The directional claim (fewer pilgrims after his preaching) is attested in this literature; a precise magnitude is not quantified.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: There was a falling off in the offerings, and consequently in the salary of Zwingle, which was drawn from them.
claims.claim_text: Offerings at the Einsiedeln shrine declined, which in turn reduced Zwingli's salary, since it was drawn from those offerings.

verification.rationale: This is a narrative detail from D'Aubigne (White's cited source) rather than an independently corroborated financial datum. It is plausible in context — pilgrimage offerings funded the clergy — but I found no independent source quantifying or confirming a fall in Zwingli's Einsiedeln salary. Not contradicted; supported at low confidence on the strength of the period source.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli in Einsiedeln

claims.quote: The authorities of the church were not blind to the work which Zwingle was accomplishing; but for the present they forbore to interfere. Hoping yet to secure him to their cause, they endeavored to win him by flatteries; and meanwhile the truth was gaining a hold upon the hearts of the people.
claims.claim_text: Church authorities, aware of Zwingli's growing influence at Einsiedeln, chose not to interfere directly but instead tried to win him over with flattery.

verification.rationale: In the early period Zwingli enjoyed protection and even favor from church figures — the papal legate courted him and he held a papal chaplaincy/pension, and the bishop did not move against him at first. The claim that authorities forbore to interfere and sought to keep him on their side is consistent with the record; 'flatteries' is D'Aubigne's coloring.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: After three years here, he was called to the office of preacher in the cathedral at Zurich.
claims.claim_text: After three years at Einsiedeln, Zwingli was called to the office of preacher in the cathedral at Zurich.

verification.rationale: Zwingli served at Einsiedeln from 1516; the Grossmunster canons elected him stipendiary priest on 11 December 1518 and he moved to Zurich on 27 December 1518, beginning to preach 1 January 1519. About three years at Einsiedeln (1516-1518) is accurate.

claims.quote: This was then the most important town of the Swiss confederacy, and the influence exerted here would be widely felt.
claims.claim_text: Zurich was then the most important town of the Swiss confederacy.

verification.rationale: Zurich was one of the foremost cities of the Confederation and a leading political/commercial center, so calling it 'most important' is a defensible period characterization (it traces to D'Aubigne). Strictly, Bern was territorially the largest and militarily the most powerful canton, so 'the most important' is a soft superlative rather than an uncontested fact; it is not clearly erroneous.

claims.quote: “You will make every exertion,” they said, “to collect the revenues of the chapter, without overlooking the least. You will exhort the faithful, both from the pulpit and in the confessional, to pay all tithes and dues, and to show by their offerings their affection to the church. You will be diligent in increasing the income arising from the sick, from masses, and in general from every ecclesiastical ordinance.”(248)
claims.claim_text: The Zurich ecclesiastics who invited Zwingli instructed him to focus his duties on collecting church revenues, tithes, and income from masses and sick calls.

verification.rationale: The charge to the new Leutpriester reaches GC through D'Aubigne (footnote 248). Its substance — the chapter's concern that the stipendiary priest secure tithes, dues, mass-offerings and revenue from the sick — reflects the real financial structure and priorities of the Grossmunster chapter. Wording is D'Aubigne's; the picture is consistent with the office.

claims.quote: “As for the administration of the sacraments, the preaching, and the care of the flock,” added his instructors, “these are also the duties of the chaplain. But for these you may employ a substitute, and particularly in preaching. You should administer the sacraments to none but persons of note, and only when called upon; you are forbidden to do so without distinction of persons.”(248)
claims.claim_text: The Zurich ecclesiastics instructed Zwingli that he could employ a substitute for preaching and administering sacraments, and should administer sacraments only to persons of note.

verification.rationale: Continuation of the same charge (footnote 248), from D'Aubigne. The permission to use a substitute (vicar) for preaching/pastoral care reflects genuine late-medieval benefice practice, where the beneficed priest often delegated cure of souls. Wording is D'Aubigne's; substance is consistent with the period's benefice system.

claims.quote: “The life of Christ,” he said, “has been too long hidden from the people. I shall preach upon the whole of the Gospel of St. Matthew, ... drawing solely from the fountains of Scripture, sounding its depths, comparing one passage with another, and seeking for understanding by constant and earnest prayer. It is to God’s glory, to the praise of His only Son, to the real salvation of souls, and to their edification in the true faith, that I shall consecrate my ministry.”(249)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli replied to his Zurich instructors that he intended to preach through the entire Gospel of Matthew, drawing solely from Scripture.

verification.rationale: Well documented: beginning 1 January 1519 Zwingli abandoned the assigned Sunday pericopes and preached continuously (lectio continua) through the Gospel of Matthew, using Erasmus's Greek text, then through the New Testament. His announced intent to preach the whole of Matthew from Scripture alone is historically confirmed; the exact wording is via D'Aubigne (footnote 249).

claims.quote: He declared that he was about to introduce no new method, but the old method employed by the church in earlier and purer times.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli declared that his proposed preaching method was not new but a return to the church's practice in earlier, purer times.

verification.rationale: Zwingli framed his continuous expository preaching as a recovery of the ancient/patristic practice of the church (e.g., the homiletic method of Chrysostom and Augustine), not an innovation. This self-understanding is consistent with the documented rationale for his lectio continua.

claims.quote: He began his ministry by opening the Gospels, and reading and explaining to his hearers the inspired narrative of the life, teachings, and death of Christ.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli began his Zurich ministry by reading and expounding the Gospel narratives of the life, teachings, and death of Christ.

verification.rationale: Confirmed: Zwingli inaugurated his Grossmunster ministry (1 Jan 1519) with continuous expository preaching through the Gospel of Matthew, reading and interpreting the Gospel narrative directly to the congregation.

claims.quote: “It is to Christ,” he said, “that I desire to lead you,—to Christ, the true source of salvation.”(250)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted telling his Zurich congregation that he desired to lead them to Christ, the true source of salvation.

verification.rationale: Quotation transmitted via D'Aubigne (footnote 250); its Christ-centered, salvation-in-Christ content is fully consistent with Zwingli's documented Zurich preaching. Substance attested; exact wording is D'Aubigne's rendering.

claims.quote: “This man,” they said, “is a preacher of the truth. He will be our Moses, to lead us forth from this Egyptian darkness.”(251)
claims.claim_text: Zurich hearers reportedly said of Zwingli that he was a preacher of the truth who would be their Moses, leading them out of "Egyptian darkness."

verification.rationale: An anonymous popular-reaction saying reported by D'Aubigne (footnote 251), White's cited source. It cannot be independently located or attributed to a named speaker, but it is an unsurprising rhetorical response and is not contradicted by any source. Supported at low confidence on the strength of the period source only.

claims.quote: “If we desire to gain over the wicked to Jesus Christ, we must shut our eyes against many things.”(252)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted saying that gaining over the wicked to Jesus Christ requires shutting one's eyes against many things.

verification.rationale: A short saying attributed to Zwingli via D'Aubigne (footnote 252). Its patient/pastoral sense is consistent with Zwingli's disposition described in the same context, but I could not independently locate the exact aphorism. Not contradicted; supported at low confidence on the period source.

claims.quote: But though at first his labors were received with great enthusiasm, after a time opposition arose. The monks set themselves to hinder his work and condemn his teachings.
claims.claim_text: After initial enthusiasm for Zwingli's preaching at Zurich, monks organized opposition to hinder his work and condemn his teachings.

verification.rationale: Zwingli's Zurich reforms provoked mounting opposition from the mendicant friars and conservative clergy (leading to the 1522-23 conflicts and disputations). Initial popularity followed by organized clerical opposition is well documented.

claims.quote: One Lucian was sent to Zurich with some of Luther’s writings, by a friend of the reformed faith at Basel, who suggested that the sale of these books might be a powerful means of scattering the light.
claims.claim_text: A man named Lucian was sent to Zurich carrying copies of Luther's writings, dispatched by a friend of the reformed faith in Basel.

verification.rationale: The 'friend of the reformed faith at Basel' is the humanist Beatus Rhenanus, who in 1519 had Luther's works reprinted at Basel and sent them to Zwingli, urging their circulation. The colporteur named 'Lucian' is a detail from D'Aubigne (footnote 253); the underlying event (Rhenanus sending/promoting Luther's writings to Zwingli via a book-peddler) is corroborated.

claims.quote: “Ascertain,” he wrote to Zwingle, “whether this man possesses sufficient prudence and skill; if so, let him carry from city to city, from town to town, from village to village, and even from house to house, among the Swiss, the works of Luther, and especially his exposition of the Lord’s prayer written for the laity. The more they are known, the more purchasers they will find.”(253)
claims.claim_text: The Basel correspondent wrote to Zwingli suggesting Lucian carry Luther's works, particularly his exposition of the Lord's Prayer, from town to town among the Swiss for sale.

verification.rationale: Quotation of Beatus Rhenanus's letter to Zwingli, via D'Aubigne (footnote 253). The content — urging that Luther's works, especially his exposition of the Lord's Prayer for the laity, be carried and sold across Switzerland — matches Rhenanus's documented role in disseminating Luther's early writings; Luther's 'Exposition of the Lord's Prayer' (1519) was indeed a popular lay text.

claims.quote: As men were rising up in different lands to present to the people forgiveness and justification through the blood of Christ, Rome proceeded with renewed energy to open her market throughout Christendom, offering pardon for money.
claims.claim_text: As reformers in various lands preached justification through Christ, the Roman church responded by intensifying the sale of indulgences for money throughout Christendom.

verification.rationale: The early 16th century saw large, aggressive indulgence campaigns across Christendom (the St. Peter's Basilica indulgence promoted by Tetzel in Germany and by Samson in Switzerland, tied to papal fundraising). Characterizing Rome as opening a wide 'market' offering pardon for money is polemical but rests on documented practice; the causal linkage to reform preaching is GC's framing.

sources.title: Medieval Indulgence & Martin Luther — World History Encyclopedia

claims.quote: Every sin had its price, and men were granted free license for crime, if the treasury of the church was kept well filled.
claims.claim_text: Under the indulgence system, every sin had an associated price, and payment to the church treasury was presented as securing license for the sin.
claims.extractor_note: Polemically framed but rests on a checkable historical claim about indulgence pricing practices.

verification.rationale: The checkable base is documented: graduated 'tariff' penances/fees assigned monetary amounts to categories of offense, and prices were scaled to the payer's wealth. Secular reference works record that in some pre-Trent cases indulgences were offered even for sins not yet committed and could function, in effect, as a 'license to sin' (an abuse that Trent's reforms later curbed). The strong 'free license for crime' wording is Protestant-polemical (matching White's sources Wylie/D'Aubigne), but it is not contradicted by modern secular historiography, which acknowledges the abuse; hence supported rather than contradicted or anachronistic. Note: Catholic indulgence theology proper remits temporal punishment for already-forgiven sin, not guilt, and does not authorize sin.
verification.modern_consensus: Historians confirm graduated monetary tariffs and pre-Reformation abuses (including indulgences perceived as licensing sin), while noting that official indulgence doctrine did not grant permission to sin.

sources.title: Medieval Indulgence & Martin Luther — World History Encyclopedia

claims.quote: In Germany the sale of indulgences had been committed to the Dominican friars, and was conducted by the infamous Tetzel.
claims.claim_text: In Germany, the sale of indulgences was entrusted to the Dominican friars and conducted by Johann Tetzel.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Johann Tetzel, a Dominican friar, was the notorious preacher/commissioner of the St. Peter's indulgence in Germany whose sales prompted Luther's 95 Theses (1517). Textbook Reformation fact.

claims.quote: In Switzerland the traffic was put into the hands of the Franciscans, under the control of Samson, an Italian monk.
claims.claim_text: In Switzerland, the sale of indulgences was entrusted to the Franciscans, under the control of Samson, an Italian monk.

verification.rationale: Bernhardin Samson (Sanson) was a Franciscan monk from Milan who acted as apostolic commissioner for the sale of indulgences in Switzerland from August 1518 — the 'Tetzel of Switzerland.' The order (Franciscan), his Italian origin, and his role are confirmed by scholarly sources.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: Samson had already done good service to the church, having secured immense sums from Germany and Switzerland to fill the papal treasury.
claims.claim_text: Samson had previously secured immense sums of money from Germany and Switzerland for the papal treasury before his Swiss indulgence campaign.

verification.rationale: Samson collected very large sums in Switzerland (Schaff describes him returning to Italy with a 'heavy, three-horse wagon of gold'), so 'immense sums ... to fill the papal treasury' is well grounded for Switzerland. The 'Germany' component is weakly attested — his major documented campaign was Swiss — so the joint 'Germany and Switzerland' claim is only partly corroborated.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: Now he traversed Switzerland, attracting great crowds, despoiling the poor peasants of their scanty earnings, and exacting rich gifts from the wealthy classes.
claims.claim_text: Samson traveled through Switzerland selling indulgences, drawing large crowds and taking money from both poor peasants and wealthy classes.

verification.rationale: Confirmed: Samson traversed Switzerland selling indulgences, giving preference to the rich (parchment letters for a crown) while selling cheaper paper letters to the poor for a few coppers, drawing large crowds. GC's description matches Schaff's account closely.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: Zwingle was still at Einsiedeln when Samson, soon after entering Switzerland, arrived with his wares at a neighboring town.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli was still stationed at Einsiedeln when Samson arrived with indulgences at a nearby town, soon after entering Switzerland.

verification.rationale: Timing is consistent: Samson entered Switzerland via the St. Gotthard in August 1518, and Zwingli remained at Einsiedeln until late December 1518. So Zwingli could indeed have been at Einsiedeln when Samson's campaign reached the region. The 'neighboring town' detail is D'Aubigne's; the broad chronology is corroborated.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: Being apprised of his mission, the Reformer immediately set out to oppose him. The two did not meet, but such was Zwingle’s success in exposing the friar’s pretensions that he was obliged to leave for other quarters.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli, on learning of Samson's mission, acted to oppose him and succeeded in exposing his pretensions, forcing Samson to leave the area, though the two never met in person.

verification.rationale: Zwingli had long been convinced of the error of indulgences and exerted his influence against Samson, contributing to his being stopped and driven off. The specific detail that 'the two did not meet' and that Zwingli forced him from a locality is D'Aubigne's narrative; the substance (Zwingli's effective opposition) is corroborated by Schaff.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: At Zurich, Zwingle preached zealously against the pardon-mongers; and when Samson approached the place, he was met by a messenger from the council, with an intimation that he was expected to pass on.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli preached at Zurich against indulgence sellers, and when Samson approached Zurich he was met by a council messenger indicating he should move on.

verification.rationale: In Zurich Samson's campaign was stopped; Zwingli preached against indulgences and the Zurich magistracy (and even the bishop of Constance) opposed him, so that he was turned back. The specific 'messenger from the council' detail is D'Aubigne's; the substance is corroborated by Schaff.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: He finally secured an entrance by stratagem, but was sent away without the sale of a single pardon, and he soon after left Switzerland.
claims.claim_text: Samson entered Zurich by stratagem but left without selling a single pardon, and soon afterward departed Switzerland.

verification.rationale: Samson was stopped in his career at Zurich and was obliged to return to Italy; Leo X issued a brief in April 1519 offering to recall and punish him if he had transgressed his authority. That he left Switzerland having been rebuffed is confirmed; the 'entrance by stratagem, not a single pardon sold' particulars come from D'Aubigne.

sources.title: Schaff, History of the Christian Church Vol. VIII: Zwingli and the Sale of Indulgences

claims.quote: A strong impetus was given to the reform by the appearance of the plague, or “great death,” which swept over Switzerland in the year 1519.
claims.claim_text: A plague known as the "great death" swept over Switzerland in 1519, giving impetus to the Reformation.

verification.rationale: A severe plague struck Zurich beginning on St. Lawrence's Day (10 August) 1519, killing roughly one in four of the city's population; it is commonly called the 'great death.' Date and event confirmed.

claims.quote: Zwingle at Zurich was smitten down; he was brought so low that all hope of his recovery was relinquished, and the report was widely circulated that he was dead.
claims.claim_text: Zwingli was struck down by the 1519 plague at Zurich and brought so low that his recovery was despaired of, and a report circulated that he had died.

verification.rationale: Zwingli remained in Zurich during the outbreak, caught the plague in September 1519, and nearly died — reduced to a feeble, bedridden state clinging to life, which he described in his three-part 'Pestlied.' The near-fatal illness is well documented; the specific 'report that he was dead' is a narrative flourish consistent with his desperate condition.

claims.quote: When he came back from the gates of death, it was to preach the gospel with greater fervor than ever before; and his words exerted an unwonted power.
claims.claim_text: After recovering from the plague, Zwingli preached the gospel with greater fervor than before, and his preaching exerted unusual power.

verification.rationale: Historians note that Zwingli's brush with death in 1519 deepened his sense of dependence on God and is thought to have strengthened his resolve to press the reform in Switzerland. The claim that he resumed with heightened fervor is consistent with this scholarly assessment (the interior 'power' is an interpretive judgment).

claims.quote: “In Adam,” he said, “we are all dead, sunk in corruption and condemnation.”(254)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted teaching that in Adam, all humanity is dead, sunk in corruption and condemnation.

verification.rationale: Quotation via D'Aubigne (footnote 254). The doctrine — universal death and condemnation in Adam — is standard Reformation soteriology and consistent with Zwingli's teaching on original sin; substance attested, wording is the period rendering.

claims.quote: “Christ ... has purchased for us a never-ending redemption ... His passion is ... an eternal sacrifice, and everlastingly effectual to heal; it satisfies the divine justice forever in behalf of all those who rely upon it with firm and unshaken faith.”
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted teaching that Christ's passion is an eternal sacrifice that satisfies divine justice forever for those who rely on it with unshaken faith.

verification.rationale: Continuation of the same passage (footnote 254/255 block), via D'Aubigne. Its content — Christ's once-for-all, eternally sufficient sacrifice received by faith — is central to Zwingli's soteriology and consistent with his documented teaching.

claims.quote: “Wherever there is faith in God, there God is; and wherever God abideth, there a zeal exists urging and impelling men to good works.”(255)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted teaching that wherever there is faith in God, a zeal exists impelling men to good works.

verification.rationale: Via D'Aubigne (footnote 255). The teaching that true faith necessarily produces good works is standard in Zwingli's theology (faith active in love/works), so the substance is consistent with his documented views.

claims.quote: Such was the interest in Zwingle’s preaching that the cathedral was filled to overflowing with the crowds that came to listen to him.
claims.claim_text: Interest in Zwingli's preaching at Zurich was such that the cathedral was filled to overflowing with listeners.

verification.rationale: Zwingli's expository preaching at the Grossmunster drew large audiences from all social classes and was a major factor in Zurich's reform. That the cathedral drew overflowing crowds is consistent with the documented popular impact of his preaching.

claims.quote: One year before, the monk of Wittenberg had uttered his “No” to the pope and the emperor at Worms, and now everything seemed to indicate a similar withstanding of the papal claims at Zurich.
claims.claim_text: One year before these events at Zurich, Martin Luther had defied the pope and the emperor at the Diet of Worms.

verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] Luther refused to recant before Emperor Charles V at the Diet of Worms in April 1521. The Zurich conflict with the bishop of Constance's deputies (1522) followed about a year later, matching GC's 'one year before.' Textbook Reformation fact.

claims.quote: Accordingly the bishop of Constance dispatched three deputies to the Council of Zurich, accusing Zwingle of teaching the people to transgress the laws of the church, thus endangering the peace and good order of society.
claims.claim_text: The bishop of Constance sent three deputies to the Council of Zurich to accuse Zwingli of teaching the people to transgress church law and endanger social order.

verification.rationale: The bishop of Constance (Hugo von Hohenlandenberg) sent a deputation to Zurich in 1522 to check Zwingli's reforms (over the Lenten fasting/'sausage' controversy and clerical celibacy), and Constance repeatedly moved against Zwingli's teaching. The precise number 'three deputies' is a detail from D'Aubigne; the underlying Constance-Zurich confrontation of 1522 is documented.

claims.quote: Zwingle replied that he had been for four years teaching the gospel in Zurich, “which was more quiet and peaceful than any other town in the confederacy.”
claims.claim_text: Zwingli replied to the bishop's deputies that he had been teaching the gospel in Zurich for four years, and that Zurich was more quiet and peaceful than any other town in the confederacy.

verification.rationale: Reported via D'Aubigne. Zwingli began preaching in Zurich on 1 January 1519, so a statement of having taught there 'four years' fits a c. 1522-23 confrontation. The claim that Zurich was orderly under his ministry is consistent with his defense; exact wording is the period rendering.

claims.quote: “Is not, then,” he said, “Christianity the best safeguard of the general security?”(256)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted asking the deputies whether Christianity was not the best safeguard of general security.

verification.rationale: A rhetorical line reported by D'Aubigne (footnote 256), White's cited source. It cannot be independently located, but it is consistent with Zwingli's argument that gospel preaching preserves rather than endangers public order. Not contradicted; supported at low confidence on the period source.

claims.quote: “Let not this accusation move you. The foundation of the church is the same Rock, the same Christ, that gave Peter his name because he confessed Him faithfully. In every nation whosoever believes with all his heart in the Lord Jesus is accepted of God. Here, truly, is the church, out of which no one can be saved.”(257)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted responding to the bishop's deputies that the foundation of the church is Christ himself, and whoever believes in the Lord Jesus is accepted of God regardless of nation.

verification.rationale: Via D'Aubigne (footnote 257). The ecclesiology — Christ (not Peter/the papacy) as the church's foundation, the church as the community of true believers of every nation — is a core Zwinglian/Reformation position, so the substance is consistent with his documented teaching.

claims.quote: As a result of the conference, one of the bishop’s deputies accepted the reformed faith.
claims.claim_text: As a result of the conference with the Council of Zurich, one of the bishop of Constance's deputies accepted the reformed faith.

verification.rationale: The Constance deputation to Zurich is documented, but the specific claim that one deputy converted to the reformed faith is a narrative detail from D'Aubigne (White's cited source) that I could not independently corroborate. It is not contradicted by any source; supported at low confidence resting on the period source only.

claims.quote: The council declined to take action against Zwingle, and Rome prepared for a fresh attack.
claims.claim_text: The Council of Zurich declined to take action against Zwingli, prompting Rome to prepare a fresh attack against him.

verification.rationale: The Zurich city council consistently backed Zwingli against the bishop of Constance (it took over governance of the church in Zurich and endorsed his reforms in the 1523 disputations), while Catholic authorities continued to seek his condemnation (culminating in the 1526 Baden disputation). The claim aligns with the documented sequence.

claims.quote: “Let them come on; I fear them as the beetling cliff fears the waves that thunder at its feet.”(258)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli is quoted saying he feared his enemies as little as a beetling cliff fears the waves thundering at its feet.

verification.rationale: A defiant saying attributed to Zwingli via D'Aubigne (footnote 258). It cannot be independently located, but it fits his documented boldness toward Rome. Not contradicted; supported at low confidence on the strength of the period source.

claims.quote: In Germany its adherents, cast down by Luther’s disappearance, took heart again, as they saw the progress of the gospel in Switzerland.
claims.claim_text: Reform adherents in Germany, discouraged by Luther's disappearance, were encouraged again upon seeing the progress of the Reformation in Switzerland.

verification.rationale: After Worms (1521) Luther was hidden at the Wartburg, and his 'disappearance' caused anxiety among German evangelicals — this framing is accurate. The claim that they took heart specifically from Swiss progress is D'Aubigne's morale interpretation, plausible but not independently quantified. Supported at low confidence.

claims.quote: “Peace has her habitation in our town,” wrote Zwingle; “no quarrel, no hypocrisy, no envy, no strife. Whence can such union come but from the Lord, and our doctrine, which fills us with the fruits of peace and piety?”(259)
claims.claim_text: Zwingli wrote that peace, free of quarrel, hypocrisy, envy, and strife, dwelt in Zurich as a fruit of the reformed doctrine.

verification.rationale: Quotation via D'Aubigne (footnote 259). It reflects Zwingli's characteristic argument that the reform brought civic peace and piety to Zurich. Cannot be independently located; supported at low confidence on the period source.

claims.quote: They would hold a disputation with Zwingle, and having the arrangement of matters, they would make sure of victory by choosing, themselves, not only the place of the combat, but the judges that should decide between the disputants.
claims.claim_text: The Romanists planned a public disputation with Zwingli and arranged to choose both the location and the judges themselves to ensure victory.

verification.rationale: The Catholic cantons convened the 1526 Baden disputation on terms designed to favor Rome — it was held at Baden (a Catholic-controlled venue rather than reform-friendly Zurich) with judges/arbiters drawn from the Confederate diet, and its outcome condemned the reformers. GC's characterization that the papal side arranged the place and judges to secure victory matches the documented setup.

claims.quote: The disputation was appointed to be held at Baden; but Zwingle was not present.
claims.claim_text: A public disputation on the Reformation was held at Baden, but Zwingli did not attend.

verification.rationale: The Baden Disputation (21 May – 8 June 1526) was held at Baden, and Zwingli deliberately did not attend, deeming it unsafe. Confirmed.

claims.quote: The Council of Zurich, suspecting the designs of the papists, and warned by the burning piles kindled in the papal cantons for confessors of the gospel, forbade their pastor to expose himself to this peril.
claims.claim_text: The Council of Zurich, suspecting the papists' designs and citing executions of gospel confessors in the papal cantons, forbade Zwingli from attending the Baden disputation.

verification.rationale: The Zurich city council, judging that Zwingli risked arrest or execution at Baden (in Catholic territory), kept him from attending; he was excommunicated in absentia at the disputation. Evangelicals had indeed been executed in Catholic cantons, so the council's fear of the 'burning piles' is grounded. Confirmed.

claims.quote: Œcolampadius and Haller were chosen to represent the Reformers, while the famous Doctor Eck, supported by a host of learned doctors and prelates, was the champion of Rome.
claims.claim_text: At the Baden disputation, Œcolampadius and Haller represented the Reformers, while Doctor Johann Eck, supported by many learned doctors and prelates, represented Rome.

verification.rationale: Confirmed: Johannes Oecolampadius (of Basel, in Zwingli's stead) and Berchtold Haller (of Bern) were the leading reform delegates at Baden, while the Catholic case was led by the theologian Johann Eck, backed by a large Catholic contingent of doctors and prelates.

claims.quote: The secretaries were all chosen by the papists, and others were forbidden to take notes, on pain of death.
claims.claim_text: At the Baden disputation, all official secretaries were chosen by the papists, and others were forbidden, under penalty of death, from taking notes.

verification.rationale: The Catholic organizers controlled the official record of the Baden disputation (the acts were later edited/published on the Catholic side, e.g. by Thomas Murner), and note-taking was restricted — which is precisely why Zwingli's supporters resorted to a clandestine student transcriber. The specific 'on pain of death' penalty is a detail from D'Aubigne/Wylie; the controlled-documentation substance is corroborated.

claims.quote: A student in attendance at the disputation, made a record each evening of the arguments that day presented. These papers two other students undertook to deliver, with the daily letters of Œcolampadius, to Zwingle at Zurich.
claims.claim_text: A student at the Baden disputation recorded the day's arguments each evening, and two other students delivered these records along with Œcolampadius's daily letters to Zwingli at Zurich.

verification.rationale: Confirmed: though kept away, Zwingli influenced Baden through daily correspondence; a student who attended the debates memorized and transcribed them, and messengers carried the notes together with Oecolampadius's letters to Zwingli, who sent back daily advice.

claims.quote: To elude the vigilance of the guard stationed at the city gates, these messengers brought baskets of poultry on their heads, and they were permitted to pass without hindrance.
claims.claim_text: The student messengers evaded guards at the city gates between Baden and Zurich by carrying baskets of poultry on their heads.

verification.rationale: The gates of Baden were guarded by soldiers and the couriers repeatedly slipped past them undetected — this is confirmed. The picturesque specific of carrying 'baskets of poultry' as the disguise is a detail from D'Aubigne (White's source); the core smuggling of notes past the guards is corroborated.

claims.quote: He “has labored more,” said Myconius, “by his meditations, his sleepless nights, and the advice which he transmitted to Baden, than he would have done by discussing in person in the midst of his enemies.”(260)
claims.claim_text: Myconius said that Zwingli labored more through his meditations, sleepless nights, and advice sent to Baden than he would have by debating in person among his enemies.

verification.rationale: Attributed to Oswald Myconius (Zwingli's friend and biographer) via D'Aubigne (footnote 260). The judgment matches the documented reality that Zwingli directed the reform side at Baden from Zurich by intensive daily correspondence rather than in person; substance consistent, wording from the period source.

claims.quote: The Romanists, flushed with anticipated triumph, had come to Baden attired in their richest robes and glittering with jewels. They fared luxuriously, their tables spread with the most costly delicacies and the choicest wines.
claims.claim_text: The Romanist delegation at the Baden disputation dressed in rich robes and jewels and dined luxuriously on costly delicacies and fine wines.

verification.rationale: A vivid contrast drawn from D'Aubigne/Wylie (White's cited sources), portraying the wealthy Catholic prelates against the plain reformers. The large, well-appointed Catholic contingent is historically plausible, but the specific luxury details are a period-source characterization not independently corroborated. Supported at low confidence.

claims.quote: In marked contrast appeared the Reformers, who were looked upon by the people as little better than a company of beggars, and whose frugal fare kept them but short time at table.
claims.claim_text: The Reformers at the Baden disputation were regarded by onlookers as little better than beggars, and ate frugally.

verification.rationale: Counterpart to the preceding contrast, from D'Aubigne/Wylie. The plainness of the reform delegates is consistent with their ethos, but the specific 'company of beggars' perception and frugal-table detail is a period-source flourish not independently verified. Supported at low confidence.

claims.quote: Œcolampadius’s landlord, taking occasion to watch him in his room, found him always engaged in study or at prayer, and greatly wondering, reported that the heretic was at least “very pious.”
claims.claim_text: Œcolampadius's landlord at Baden observed him constantly studying or praying and reported that the "heretic" was at least very pious.

verification.rationale: An anecdote from D'Aubigne (White's cited source) about Oecolampadius's piety impressing his host at Baden. It is consistent with Oecolampadius's studious, devout reputation, but the specific incident cannot be independently corroborated. Not contradicted; supported at low confidence on the period source.

claims.quote: “Eck haughtily ascended a pulpit splendidly decorated, while the humble Œcolampadius, meanly clothed, was forced to take his seat in front of his opponent on a rudely carved stool.”(261)
claims.claim_text: At the Baden disputation, Eck ascended a splendidly decorated pulpit while Œcolampadius, meanly clothed, sat on a rudely carved stool facing him.

verification.rationale: This quotation is footnoted (261) to Wylie's 'History of Protestantism,' White's cited source. It dramatizes the staging of the debate to Eck's advantage. The unequal, Catholic-favoring arrangement of the Baden disputation is historically attested; the specific pulpit/stool tableau is Wylie's period rendering.

claims.quote: His zeal was stimulated by the hope of gold as well as fame; for the defender of the faith was to be rewarded by a handsome fee.
claims.claim_text: Eck's zeal at the Baden disputation was stimulated by the prospect of a handsome monetary fee as well as fame.

verification.rationale: The imputation of a financial motive (a fee for the 'defender of the faith') to Eck comes from D'Aubigne/Wylie, White's cited sources. Eck was a professional controversialist rewarded for his services to the Catholic cause, so the claim is plausible, but the specific 'handsome fee' at Baden is a period-source characterization not independently confirmed. Supported at low confidence.

claims.quote: he entered upon it with the solemn avowal, “I acknowledge no other standard of judgment than the word of God.”(262)
claims.claim_text: Œcolampadius opened the Baden disputation with the avowal that he acknowledged no standard of judgment other than the word of God.

verification.rationale: Via D'Aubigne (footnote 262). The sola-scriptura stance — accepting only the Word of God as the standard — was precisely the reform delegates' declared basis at Baden (the reformers refused to admit church tradition/authority as arbiter), so the avowal is consistent with Oecolampadius's documented position.

claims.quote: “Custom,” he said, “has no force in our Switzerland, unless it be according to the constitution; now, in matters of faith, the Bible is our constitution.”(263)
claims.claim_text: Œcolampadius argued at Baden that in Switzerland custom has no force unless it accords with the constitution, and that in matters of faith the Bible is that constitution.

verification.rationale: Via D'Aubigne (footnote 263). The argument — that custom/tradition yields to Scripture as the supreme rule ('constitution') in matters of faith — is a direct expression of the reform delegates' sola-scriptura case at Baden and consistent with Oecolampadius's documented theology.

claims.quote: The contrast between the two disputants was not without effect. The calm, clear reasoning of the Reformer, so gently and modestly presented, appealed to minds that turned in disgust from Eck’s boastful and boisterous assumptions.
claims.claim_text: The contrast between Œcolampadius's calm, modest reasoning and Eck's boastful manner at Baden influenced onlookers, turning some in favor of the Reformers.

verification.rationale: Control-sample re-check of an extractor 'needs_verification:false' claim. This is an interpretive/rhetorical judgment about the persuasive effect of the two men's demeanor on unnamed listeners. Its factual kernel (that Baden's outcome, despite the formal Catholic verdict, ultimately advanced the reform) is treated elsewhere (GC-09-096); the demeanor-and-effect characterization here is not independently checkable. Correctly left unverified.

claims.quote: The discussion continued eighteen days.
claims.claim_text: The Baden disputation continued for eighteen days.

verification.rationale: The Baden Disputation ran from 21 May to 8 June 1526 — about eighteen days, as commonly stated. Confirmed.

claims.quote: At its close, the papists with great confidence claimed the victory. Most of the deputies sided with Rome, and the Diet pronounced the Reformers vanquished, and declared that they, together with Zwingle, their leader, were cut off from the church.
claims.claim_text: At the close of the Baden disputation, most deputies sided with Rome, and the Diet declared the Reformers, along with Zwingli, cut off from the church.

verification.rationale: Confirmed: at Baden the Catholic party claimed victory, a majority of the Confederate deputies sided with Rome, all innovations were forbidden, Zwingli was excommunicated, and Basel was called on to depose Oecolampadius. GC's summary matches the documented result.

claims.quote: But the fruits of the conference revealed on which side the advantage lay. The contest resulted in a strong impetus to the Protestant cause, and it was not long afterward that the important cities of Bern and Basel declared for the Reformation.
claims.claim_text: Not long after the Baden disputation, the cities of Bern and Basel declared for the Reformation.

verification.rationale: Despite the formal Catholic victory at Baden (1526), the disputation drew many toward the reform; Bern officially adopted the Reformation in 1528 (after the Bern Disputation) and Basel in 1529. GC's 'not long afterward ... Bern and Basel declared for the Reformation' is accurate (a 2-3 year interval).
